item.description: The diplomatic-intelligence paradox describes the structural tension that emerges when the intelligence apparatus simultaneously supports diplomatic engagement with an adversary and operational planning for military action against the same adversary. The 2026 Iran war presents this paradox with unusual clarity: U.S.-Iran negotiations over Iran’s nuclear program proceeded through indirect talks in Oman (6 February 2026) and direct negotiations in Geneva, while the CIA was simultaneously conducting months of surveillance of Ali Khamenei’s movements in preparation for the strike campaign. On 27 February — one day before the strikes — Oman’s foreign minister announced that a “breakthrough” had been reached and Iran had agreed to halt uranium enrichment and accept full IAEA verification.

Operation Epic Fury is the United States military designation for its component of the joint U.S.-Israeli strike campaign against Iran, initiated on 28 February 2026. The operation is executed under U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM) authority and encompasses air strikes using B-2, B-1, and B-52 strategic bombers; Tomahawk cruise missile launches from naval vessels; HIMARS ground-launched precision strikes; and the employment of undisclosed long-range standoff weapons. The stated objectives include the destruction of Iran’s ballistic missile infrastructure, nuclear program facilities, IRGC command structures, and naval capabilities.

Operation Roaring Lion is the Israeli military designation for its component of the joint U.S.-Israeli strike campaign against Iran, initiated on 28 February 2026. The IDF stated that it struck 500 Iranian targets in the first wave. Israel reportedly launched the strike on Khamenei’s compound unilaterally, using U.S. intelligence — a detail that, if accurate, reveals the operational division of labor between Israeli strike execution and American intelligence support in the decapitation operation.

Operation True Promise IV is Iran’s designation for its retaliatory operations following the U.S.-Israeli strikes of 28 February 2026. The name continues the “True Promise” sequence from prior Iranian retaliatory operations against Israel, asserting continuity with previous rounds of escalation.

The operation encompassed ballistic missile and drone attacks against U.S. military installations in Jordan, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Iraq, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE; strikes on civilian airports and shipping ports across the Gulf; the closure of the Strait of Hormuz to commercial shipping; and attacks on energy infrastructure, including strikes on Qatar’s LNG facilities. The multi-domain, multi-theater character of the response — striking across the entire Persian Gulf rather than concentrating on the adversary’s military assets — reflects an asymmetric escalation strategy that targets the coalition’s political cohesion by imposing costs on regional states hosting U.S. forces.

The Strait of Hormuz closure refers to the IRGC’s progressive shutdown of commercial shipping through the strait beginning 28 February 2026, in retaliation for the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran. The closure escalated from initial warnings to a 70% reduction in traffic by 1 March, followed by an IRGC official’s confirmation on 2 March that the strait was closed, with the threat that any vessel transiting would be “set ablaze.”

The closure halted approximately 20% of global oil supplies, driving Brent crude prices from approximately $70 to over $80 per barrel within days, with analyst forecasts of $100 or higher if disruptions persist. European natural gas prices doubled from €30/MWh to over €60/MWh. The economic disruption extends beyond energy: major container shipping companies including Maersk and Hapag-Lloyd suspended transits through the strait and related Red Sea routes, rerouting around the Cape of Good Hope.
